artifacts.description: CSV containing quantitative counts extracted from the accessible pediatric RNS PubMed abstract.
Bar chart summarizing extracted cohort-level counts from the accessible pediatric RNS abstract.
Run log documenting the search process, extraction steps, and why original sex-stratified analysis was not possible.

limitations: No public downloadable patient-level dataset for pediatric RNS outcomes by sex was identified in this run.
The accessible PubMed source provided only abstract-level summary information, not raw or tabulated sex-stratified outcomes.
Without individual-level or at least sex-stratified aggregate data, original statistical estimation of sex differences was not possible.
The result is a feasibility and provenance assessment rather than a completed sex-difference efficacy study.
methods: I searched the web for public data sources specifically related to pediatric epilepsy, responsive neurostimulation (RNS), and sex differences. I reviewed the most relevant accessible result, a 2024 PubMed record for a systematic review and individual patient meta-analysis in pediatric RNS (PMID 39060746), because it directly addressed pediatric RNS and mentioned sex as a variable. From the accessible abstract text, I extracted only quantitative cohort-level counts explicitly visible in the source (105 aggregated pediatric patients and 17 retrospective studies) and saved them as a CSV table. I also generated a simple bar figure summarizing those extracted counts and saved a text log documenting the workflow. I did not perform a sex-stratified outcome analysis because no downloadable public patient-level dataset for pediatric RNS outcomes by sex was identified in this run, and the accessible source did not expose the underlying individual-level data needed for original computation.
research_question: Can sex differences in treatment response among pediatric patients receiving responsive neurostimulation (RNS) for drug-resistant epilepsy be evaluated with publicly accessible patient-level data discovered in this run?

results.text: The run did not identify a public patient-level dataset that would allow an original sex-stratified analysis of pediatric RNS outcomes. The most directly relevant accessible source was a 2024 pediatric RNS systematic review abstract reporting 105 aggregated pediatric patients from 17 retrospective studies and stating that RNS effectiveness was not influenced by patient sex, but the underlying analyzable individual-level data were not publicly available from the accessed material. Therefore, no original inferential test of sex differences could be carried out in this run.

api_query.search_query: responsive neurostimulation pediatric epilepsy sex differences

sources.label: PubMed record for pediatric RNS systematic review and individual patient meta-analysis
sources.notes: Accessible abstract reported 17 retrospective studies and 105 aggregated pediatric patients; it stated that effectiveness was not influenced by patient sex, but no downloadable patient-level data were exposed in the accessed material.

title: Feasibility assessment of studying sex differences in pediatric epilepsy among RNS-treated patients using publicly accessible data
